finding_statement: Autonomous loop performs write actions with weak approval framing
stage2_rationale_short: OpenAI Codex babysit-pr skill explicitly instructs continuous polling, per-loop patch/commit/push, and auto-resolution of review comments without per-action user confirmation.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The skill's frontmatter describes 'continuously polling review comments, CI checks/workflow runs, and mergeability state until the PR is merged/closed or user help is required ... auto-fix/push branch-related issues when appropriate.' The 15-step core workflow tells the agent to patch code locally, commit, and push on every loop iteration, with retries and auto-resolution. Stop conditions exist: 'user help required' branches for CI infrastructure issues, exhausted retries, permissions, or ambiguity, plus 'If unrelated uncommitted changes are present, stop and ask the user' (line 110), and a hard 3-retry budget for flaky failures. The default-autonomous direction (line 139: 'Do not stop to ask the user whether to continue polling') is real, but the surface is narrowly scoped to a single PR branch the user explicitly asked the agent to monitor. On re-audit, harm relevance is more accurately 'medium' than 'high' given the explicit stop-and-ask branches.
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: Stop conditions and a 3-retry budget for flaky failures provide some bounding.
stage2_notes: harm_relevance corrected from 'high' to 'medium' after re-audit noted multiple stop-and-ask branches.

stage2_rationale_detailed: The skill's content includes `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/` (AWS IMDSv1 credentials), `http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/` (GCP metadata), Redis localhost URLs, and SSRF bypass variants (octal/hex/decimal IP encoding, xip.io, redirect chains). This is operational SSRF/credential-extraction tooling. Marked as offensive but no scoping/authorization gate exists in the file itself.
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: Skill is framed as pentesting; users are expected to have authorization, but this is not enforced in-skill.

finding_statement: Broad external-action surface without approval model
stage2_rationale_short: Snippet is an LLM-tooling inventory listing (Claude SDK, Context7, MCP servers, document-processing libraries), not action instructions.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The matched text is a curated list of recommended tools per integration category — Claude SDK for LLM integration, MCP servers for prompt management, unstructured/pdfplumber/mammoth for document processing. Like 0089, this is a recommendation/inventory document, not a broad-action surface. The detector counted ras=3 on the names of these integrations.

finding_statement: Autonomous loop performs write actions with weak approval framing
stage2_rationale_short: fix-ci-failures skill is a one-shot diagnose-and-fix workflow, not an autonomous loop.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The skill describes a 5-step linear workflow: identify branch, check CI, download logs, extract failure, fix and push. It is not a continuous polling loop and the writes are gated on the agent diagnosing a single failure. The detector counted 'commit/push' write markers and a single 'until green' phrase, but the operational meaning is that the agent fixes one failure and pushes once, not that it runs an unattended loop. The autonomy framing is weak compared to genuine loop skills like pr-address.
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: The 5-step workflow is sequential and one-shot rather than continuous.

finding_statement: Autonomous loop performs write actions with weak approval framing
stage2_rationale_short: gsd-execute-phase orchestrator spawns parallel subagents in waves and executes plans without per-action gating; --interactive is opt-in, not default.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The skill is a wave-based parallel-execution orchestrator: it discovers plans, analyzes dependencies, groups into waves, and spawns subagents that execute the plans. The default mode is non-interactive — --interactive is an explicit flag. Each subagent loads its own execute-plan context and writes code. There is no per-action user-confirmation gate in the default flow. This is a clear autonomous-loop-with-writes pattern, with the additional concern of subagent fan-out.
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: An --interactive mode exists for stepwise execution, but it is opt-in.

finding_statement: [collection-scale] Agent Identity Spoofing and Authority Impersonation
stage2_rationale_short: Collection-scale aggregate has internal counting defects (>100% replication on multiple sub-rules) and the cross-agent-attack rule fires on plain prose mentioning 'agent' or 'instructions'.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The aggregate reports replication ratios of 460%, 293%, 53%, and 47% across cohort '/category4_surface_mentions' for several agent-manipulation rules. Replications above 100% are mathematically impossible relative to the stated denominator (15 skills) and indicate the aggregator is double-counting matches per file or misjoining example sets. The bundled example files are coherent project AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md documents (apfel Swift CLI, mcp_agent_mail, Lychee, etc.) without any cross-agent attack instructions. The underlying detector pattern overfires on routine agent-tooling prose. [SELF-AUDIT NOTE: Confidence reduced from 'high' to 'medium'. The 'X/15 skills (Y%)' notation in the aggregator is genuinely ambiguous — X may be matches across files rather than skills with at least one match, in which case >100% is not strictly impossible. The substantive FP reasoning (defensive prose, polarity inversion, irrelevant code in bundled examples) still holds for the examples I saw, but I cannot rule out that the four-or-more files for the named sub-rule include real positives I did not examine.]
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: Aggregate replication ratios over 100% and bundled examples (apfel/AGENTS.md is a normal project guide) contradict the claim.

finding_statement: Autonomous loop performs write actions with weak approval framing
stage2_rationale_short: hermes-agent research-paper skill includes 'commit early, commit often' guidance and a 5-step BibTeX fetch loop, with low approval framing relative to autonomy framing.
stage2_rationale_detailed: The skill instructs the agent to 'always fetch programmatically' for citations and to follow a mandatory multi-step process inside an iterative drafting/compiling loop. The detector flags writes (commit/push) and autonomy markers; reading the skill confirms it expects the agent to iterate over paper sections, compile, and commit without per-step user gates beyond the high-level setup. The harm relevance is moderate (writes are confined to the user's paper repo) but the autonomy-without-approval pattern is real.
stage2_disconfirming_evidence: The skill is scoped to a single research repo and the actions are narrow (LaTeX edits, git commits).
